text:
TheOlympics Opening Ceremonyis always a big event – whenLondonhosted the games in 2012, around 27 million people across the UK tuned in, alongside a further 900 million from overseas.

ThoughParishas decided to opt out of a stadium show for the first time, instead hosting it along the banks of the River Seine, anticipation is high.

But as each country’s representatives prepare to step out this evening, we couldn’t help but wonder: could the outfits selected forTeam GBhave been a little more exciting?

Rower Helen Glover and diverTom Daleywere pictured wearing the ‘fits outside the British Embassy in Paris earlier this week.

Designed by Ben Sherman, they feature cream and navy bomber-style jackets (with extra red on the cuffs and a four-nation floral design on the back) charmingly paired with a knitted vest covered in little Union Jack-style prints.

But it’s all very tame, leading some Olympics fans to question whether the design might be a little lacklustre.

‘Really don’t like the white shirts with half a Union Flag,’ @TrollyDash commented on one of Team GB’s TikTok videos. ‘The jacket is redeemed by the cool design on the back though.’

Elsewhere, Olympic bronze medallist Monica Aksamit added in a video posted to her own account: ‘The UK has been getting a lot of hate for this, and I’m just not impressed.’

Monica rated the outfit a forgettable five out of 10, describing it as ‘bland’ and ‘not giving much.’

So, with that view, here’s Metro Lifestyle’s take on everything Team GB could’ve worn or taken inspiration from for the Olympic Opening Ceremony instead, from the style of one particular Spice Girl to historical fashion.

A glaringly obvious comparison, Team GB could’ve taken inspiration fromGeri Halliwell(AKAGinger Spice) and her famous flag dress – because no matter what society says, they’re for everyone.

Geri became the poster girl for Cool Britannia when she asked her sister to stitch a Union Jack tea towel onto her black Gucci mini dress because she wanted to ‘celebrate being British.’

Wearing with red Buffalo boots while performing Who Do You Think You Are at the 1997 Brit Awards, the iconic dress was sold one year later for £41,320.

How’s that for girl power?

The Beefeater is a classic British look, and refers to the traditional dress worn by the Yeoman Warders (nicknamed ‘Beefeaters’) who have guarded the Tower of London since the Tudor Era.

They’re also personal bodyguards to King Charles (a service they’ve offered to the reigning monarch since the early 1500s).

They don’t just have one uniform, either: there’s one for state dress (comprised of a glitzy red and gold) and one known as the ‘undress’ outfit (AKA, the one with the classic dark blue hues).

No doubt, Team GB would’ve looked dashing with a few references here and there: gold-piped shorts and a sporty nylon ruff, perhaps?

The Pearly Kings and Queens tradition was borne out of working-class London society – and it combines the outlandishness of British fashion with good-hearted charity.

It was started by Victorian street-sweeper Henry Croft, who was the first to decorate his clothes with mother-of-pearl buttons (hence the name Pearly King) to draw attention to himself as he raised money on the streets.

Later on, in 1911, the first organised pearly society formed in Finchley in North London. To this day, Pearly Kings and Queens continue to raise money for causes across the capital, and there are now around 30 Pearly families.

Wouldn’t a bit of embellishment have looked fun on Team GB? UK designer Ashish would have knocked a bedazzled Opening Ceremony look out of the park.

With Team GB hailing from so many places across the country, the designers might’ve decided to kit them out in choices specific to where they’re from. Yes, we know this would’ve entailed much more work – but it’s fun in principle, right?

The very image of Scotland itself, a kilt – or even just a tartan-inspired look – would’ve looked so fresh on Team GB. They’ve been around since the 16th century, so it’s not exactly radical. Athletes from Northern Ireland are allowed to choose who they compete for (between Team GB and Ireland) and tartan trousers have been seen in traditional Northern Irish dress too.

Meanwhile, the rural women of Wales have a long history with Welsh traditional costume.

The traditional ‘Welsh Lady’ is associated with the local flannel, a woollen fabric incorporating red, green, yellow and black, as well as distinctive petticoats and black beaver hats.

To let each sportsperson show off their heritage on the international stage would’ve been a huge win for Britain – medals or no medals.

title: The outfits Team GB should have worn to the Olympics Opening Ceremony | Metro News
description: We couldn’t help but wonder: could the outfits selected for Team GB at the Paris 2024 Opening Ceremony have been a little more exciting?
